steps.conclusion: The curriculum is strong overall, but it omits two day-one JavaScript competencies that warrant explicit chapters: version control and collaborative Git workflows, and JavaScript receiver semantics (`this`, method calls, `call`, `apply`, and `bind`). CI/CD, library publishing, classes, and event handlers otherwise assume parts of this knowledge. The closing chapter covers roles, competence artifacts, and staying current, but it does not clearly teach concrete entry and contribution paths such as jobs, internships, open source, and practitioner communities.

Testing appears quite late, after server APIs, databases, authentication, and GraphQL, even though those chapters benefit from testing skills. A few chapters also bundle areas that could use sharper scopes, especially strings/numbers/dates/internationalization and Canvas/animation/media APIs.

Move core debugging and unit-testing practices earlier so learners use them during browser and server projects. Add explicit treatment of JSON parsing/serialization and version-control-based collaboration, and consider staged projects that reinforce the progression before the final end-to-end workflow.

testCase.expectations:
- MUST be in US English

      
  - Mission context: this curriculum is for learners OUTSIDE privileged environments — people who doubt they can become serious practitioners. Chapters should feel welcoming and legible, not gate-kept by academic vocabulary
  - MAJOR ERROR: the curriculum must be a serious arc from zero knowledge to mastery, not a shallow tour or a list of disconnected topics
  - MAJOR ERROR: chapters must follow a logical learning progression. Each chapter should depend only on earlier chapters and ordinary life experience a true beginner can reasonably have
  - MAJOR ERROR: the opening chapters must teach the natural foundations a true beginner needs first
  - MAJOR ERROR: practical work must appear throughout the course once prerequisites support it. A curriculum that stays purely theoretical or avoids projects, tools, cases, techniques, artifacts, and workflows is incomplete
  - MAJOR ERROR: opening with a showcase project, specialist case, lab technique, advanced tool, or dramatic scenario before the learner has the prerequisites is wrong even when the example is real and concrete
  - For chronological subjects, the chapter order must follow the real chronology. For skill-based subjects, it must follow the real skill progression. For science and engineering, observable foundations should come before specialist techniques
  - Foundational chapters are allowed and often necessary, but they must teach concrete concepts, vocabulary, chronology, notation, mental models, or simple skills the learner will use later — not just describe the field from the outside
  - Chapter count is a RESULT of coverage, not a target. Do not penalize or reward specific counts when coverage is justified. Evaluate whether every canonical pillar of the field has at least one chapter — if any pillar is missing, the curriculum is incomplete regardless of count. Broad fields, large platforms, and large fictional canons can legitimately need many chapters. Penalize duplicate, padded, or poorly sequenced chapters, not length by itself
  - MAJOR ERROR: trading breadth for recency. Modern topics must ADD coverage without REPLACING canonical foundations. For example, a machine learning curriculum that covers transformers/LLMs/RAG/diffusion/fine-tuning but drops reinforcement learning, time-series forecasting, recommender systems, or causal inference is incomplete — those are canonical ML pillars. A medicine curriculum that covers telemedicine and modern EMRs but drops anatomy, physiology, pathology, or pharmacology is incomplete. If covering everything needs more chapters, more chapters is the correct answer
  - Must cover BOTH academic AND practical pillars:
    - Academic pillars: theory, foundations, history, canonical body of knowledge
    - Modern toolchain: what working practitioners in this field actually use this year
    - End-to-end workflow: at least one chapter walking through how real work in the field gets delivered start to finish
    - Last-decade shifts: what's been added to the field in the past 10 years that a textbook wouldn't have covered 10 years ago (new regulations, new platforms, new techniques, AI changes, etc.)
  - MAJOR ERROR: for broad fields, professions, sciences, engineering subjects, and serious practices, missing a standalone history/evolution chapter is an academic-pillar gap unless the course itself is already chronological history or the subject is a narrow tool/hobby where history would be padding
  - MAJOR ERROR: for fields, professions, and serious practices, missing a standalone end-to-end workflow chapter is a practical-pillar gap. The workflow should show real work from problem/request/question through decisions, execution, validation, delivery, operation, maintenance, or follow-up, depending on the field
  - For regulated professions and fields with safety duties, local rules, required documents, reporting duties, professional responsibility, and safety practice are curriculum pillars. A generic ethics chapter is not enough when practitioners need separate procedures and judgment
  - The "junior practitioner test": if a junior would be embarrassed on day 1 of real practice because a topic is missing, that topic must exist as its own chapter
  - Cover core pillars before specialized or frontier topics
  - Stay tightly focused on the course title. Generic cross-disciplinary chapters that could be copied unchanged into many unrelated courses are a major error unless the topic IS the subject of the course
  - MAJOR ERROR: meta-chapters that describe, taxonomize, or survey the field from the outside instead of teaching it
  - MAJOR ERROR: survey chapters that list many areas as bullet points without teaching any of them — each topic that matters deserves its own chapter with real depth
  - MAJOR ERROR: catch-all "recent developments" chapters. Titles like "Catch up with the last decade of X", "What's new in X", "Recent developments in X", "The last decade of X", or "Modern X" that bundle multiple distinct modern techniques into a single chapter are a major error. Each modern shift (transformers, LLMs, RAG, diffusion, fine-tuning for ML; CRISPR, sequencing, single-cell biology, spatial omics, synthetic biology for Biology; LGPD/GDPR, digital filing for Law; telemedicine, EMRs for Medicine; etc.) deserves its own substantive chapter
  - Avoid overlap. If two chapters would naturally share most of their lessons, they should be merged or their scopes sharpened
  - Prefer concept names over vendor names in chapter titles/descriptions (e.g., "Package management" instead of a specific tool name). Foundational tools that ARE the subject of the course are an allowed exception. Vendor name usage in non-vendor courses is a minor issue, never a major error
  - For courses covering a field, profession, or serious practice, a "navigating the field" closing chapter is expected. It must cover ROLES AND SPECIALTIES (what kinds of practitioners exist and what they do differently), CONTRIBUTION/ENTRY PATHS (how people actually join — jobs, residencies, open source, communities), the ARTIFACT THAT PROVES COMPETENCE in this field (code repos, case records, clinical hours, recordings), and HOW PRACTITIONERS KEEP SKILLS CURRENT. A narrow "Build a Portfolio" or "Build a Project" closing chapter is a major error — a portfolio is just one small part of navigating a field. The closing chapter must be specific to the subject, not generic career advice
  - For hobby / pop-culture topics, no closing navigation chapter is expected
  - Titles: concise, specific, concrete, natural, learner-facing, and sentence case. Preserve normal capitalization for proper nouns, product names, acronyms, and language-specific conventions. Chapter titles should use the canonical names a serious learner would expect in a normal course outline, especially for searchable field pillars, methods, tools, eras, genres, regulations, standards, and named concepts. Canonical titles such as "Algorithms", "Data structures", "Operating systems", "Cell biology", "Civil procedure", "Backpropagation", "Retrieval-augmented generation", or their natural translated equivalents are good when those are the real chapter topics
  - MAJOR STYLE ISSUE: hiding canonical terms behind practical paraphrases, metaphors, slogans, or "what it does" titles. If a learner scanning or searching for a standard topic would not recognize the chapter because the title avoids the expected term, the title is not good enough. For example, "Informação guardada em bits" is worse than a title that names "Representação de dados"; "O que um computador faz de verdade" is worse than a title that names the actual pillar such as "Arquitetura de computadores" or "Organização de computadores"; "Catch bugs before users do" is worse than "Testing JavaScript code"
  - SCORE CAP: if 3 or more chapter titles hide important canonical/searchable terms that belong in those titles, the majorErrors score must be 7 or lower even when descriptions mention the terms and the underlying coverage is strong. If 1-2 important titles hide canonical/searchable terms, the majorErrors score must be 8 or lower. Descriptions recovering the term do not remove the title problem because users scan and search chapter titles first
  - MAJOR STYLE ISSUE: titles that read like academic catalog entries only because they are bloated, comma-separated inventories, abstract noun stacks, or title-case headlines. Do not penalize a concise canonical course title as "academic" just because it uses the standard term for the topic. Avoid vague, cute, clickbait, or slogan-like titles such as "X Without Fear" unless that phrase is the natural name of the topic. Avoid "I / II / Part 1" — use descriptive subtitles instead
  - Descriptions: 1–2 sentences describing what the chapter covers and, when natural, what it enables. Descriptions should explain the chapter's content clearly enough that a learner can tell which canonical topics, tools, cases, mechanisms, or skills belong inside it. A description that only says what the learner will feel or broadly do is too vague if it does not name the actual scope
  - Description filler issue: descriptions should not rely on filler phrases like "explore", "understand", "learn about", "introduction to", or "basics of". Repeated filler phrasing, or descriptions that use these words instead of naming concrete scope, is a minor issue.
  - Warm, plain language throughout. Avoid academic vocabulary
  - Language output must follow the language parameter. For `en`: US English. For `pt`: Brazilian Portuguese. For `es`: Latin American Spanish
  - Titles and descriptions must not leak prompt instructions or make claims about the learner's performance
  - Don't evaluate output format — focus on chapter content quality only

steps.conclusion: The curriculum has a strong zero-to-practice arc, extensive Brazilian-law coverage, a dedicated history chapter, practical work throughout, modern tools, and multiple end-to-end workflows. The main completeness gap is in constitutional and procedural practice: it lacks substantive coverage of the legislative process and the organization/functions of the Legislative and Executive branches, while “Federalismo e organização dos poderes” mainly covers federal competencies. It also omits a dedicated chapter on Juizados Especiais, a canonical and highly common entry point for Brazilian civil and criminal practice.

“Instituições do sistema de justiça” combines the Judiciary, Ministério Público, Defensoria Pública, public advocacy, police, and audit courts in one chapter; for a mastery curriculum, this risks treating several distinct institutions too briefly. The title “Federalismo e organização dos poderes” also promises broader coverage than its description provides.

Add focused chapters on processo legislativo, the constitutional roles of the Legislative and Executive branches, and Juizados Especiais Cíveis e Criminais. Coverage could also be strengthened with a dedicated treatment of Brazil’s recent tax reform and its transition rules, rather than leaving this major current shift implicit within the existing tax chapters.

steps.conclusion: The curriculum is exceptionally broad and largely coherent, but it has two material structural gaps. It lacks a standalone Clínica médica chapter integrating common adult multisystem care; organ-based specialties and hospital workflows do not fully replace that canonical pillar. In addition, invasive procedures appear before bioethics, professional rules, patient safety, infection control, and biosafety, even though consent, asepsis, incident prevention, and occupational safety are prerequisites. “Modelos contemporâneos de cuidado” also risks becoming a catch-all chapter by combining hospital-at-home, hybrid care, patient navigation, multidisciplinary teams, and risk-based coordination rather than giving distinct major models substantive treatment.

“Documentos médicos obrigatórios” is an imprecise title because not every listed document is universally mandatory, and it hides searchable duties such as atestados médicos and ordinary prescription rules. The controlled-substance chapter mentions prescription types and legal requirements but would be more practically useful if its scope explicitly named Brazilian notification forms, categories, validity, territorial rules, retention, and correction procedures. Semiologia compresses anamnese and the full physical examination into one chapter, which may be too broad for foundational clinical skills.

Add a dedicated Clínica médica chapter with common adult presentations, multimorbidity, inpatient and outpatient management, and transitions of care. Move bioethics, consent, patient safety, infection control, and biosafety before invasive procedures.   - Mission context: this curriculum is for learners OUTSIDE privileged environments — people who doubt they can become serious practitioners. Chapters should feel welcoming and legible, not gate-kept by academic vocabulary

  - MAJOR ERROR: chapters must follow a logical learning progression. Each chapter should depend only on earlier chapters and ordinary life experience a true beginner can reasonably have
  - MAJOR ERROR: the opening chapters must teach the natural foundations a true beginner needs first
  - MAJOR ERROR: practical work must appear throughout the course once prerequisites support it. A curriculum that stays purely theoretical or avoids projects, tools, cases, techniques, artifacts, and workflows is incomplete
  - MAJOR ERROR: opening with a showcase project, specialist case, lab technique, advanced tool, or dramatic scenario before the learner has the prerequisites is wrong even when the example is real and concrete

  - Foundational chapters are allowed and often necessary, but they must teach concrete concepts, vocabulary, chronology, notation, mental models, or simple skills the learner will use later — not just describe the field from the outside
  - Chapter count is a RESULT of coverage, not a target. Do not penalize or reward specific counts when coverage is justified. Evaluate whether every canonical pillar of the field has at least one chapter — if any pillar is missing, the curriculum is incomplete regardless of count. For example, a machine learning curriculum that covers transformers/LLMs/RAG/diffusion/fine-tuning but drops reinforcement learning, time-series forecasting, recommender systems, or causal inference is incomplete — those are canonical ML pillars. A medicine curriculum that covers telemedicine and modern EMRs but drops anatomy, physiology, pathology, or pharmacology is incomplete. If covering everything needs more chapters, more chapters is the correct answer
  - Must cover BOTH academic AND practical pillars:
    - Academic pillars: theory, foundations, history, canonical body of knowledge
    - Modern toolchain: what working practitioners in this field actually use this year
    - End-to-end workflow: at least one chapter walking through how real work in the field gets delivered start to finish
    - Last-decade shifts: what's been added to the field in the past 10 years that a textbook wouldn't have covered 10 years ago (new regulations, new platforms, new techniques, AI changes, etc.)
  - MAJOR ERROR: for broad fields, professions, sciences, engineering subjects, and serious practices, missing a standalone history/evolution chapter is an academic-pillar gap unless the course itself is already chronological history or the subject is a narrow tool/hobby where history would be padding
  - MAJOR ERROR: for fields, professions, and serious practices, missing a standalone end-to-end workflow chapter is a practical-pillar gap. The workflow should show real work from problem/request/question through decisions, execution, validation, delivery, operation, maintenance, or follow-up, depending on the field
  - For regulated professions and fields with safety duties, local rules, required documents, reporting duties, professional responsibility, and safety practice are curriculum pillars. A generic ethics chapter is not enough when practitioners need separate procedures and judgment
  - The "junior practitioner test": if a junior would be embarrassed on day 1 of real practice because a topic is missing, that topic must exist as its own chapter
  - Cover core pillars before specialized or frontier topics
  - Stay tightly focused on the course title. Generic cross-disciplinary chapters that could be copied unchanged into many unrelated courses are a major error unless the topic IS the subject of the course
  - MAJOR ERROR: meta-chapters that describe, taxonomize, or survey the field from the outside instead of teaching it
  - MAJOR ERROR: survey chapters that list many areas as bullet points without teaching any of them — each topic that matters deserves its own chapter with real depth
  - MAJOR ERROR: catch-all "recent developments" chapters.   - Prefer concept names over vendor names in chapter titles/descriptions (e.g., "Package management" instead of a specific tool name). Foundational tools that ARE the subject of the course are an allowed exception. Vendor name usage in non-vendor courses is a minor issue, never a major error
  - For courses covering a field, profession, or serious practice, a "navigating the field" closing chapter is expected.   - For hobby / pop-culture topics, no closing navigation chapter is expected
  - Titles: concise, specific, concrete, natural, learner-facing, and sentence case. Preserve normal capitalization for proper nouns, product names, acronyms, and language-specific conventions. Chapter titles should use the canonical names a serious learner would expect in a normal course outline, especially for searchable field pillars, methods, tools, eras, genres, regulations, standards, and named concepts. Canonical titles such as "Algorithms", "Data structures", "Operating systems", "Cell biology", "Civil procedure", "Backpropagation", "Retrieval-augmented generation", or their natural translated equivalents are good when those are the real chapter topics

If 1-2 important titles hide canonical/searchable terms, the majorErrors score must be 8 or lower. Descriptions recovering the term do not remove the title problem because users scan and search chapter titles first

Do not penalize a concise canonical course title as "academic" just because it uses the standard term for the topic.   - Descriptions: 1–2 sentences describing what the chapter covers and, when natural, what it enables. Descriptions should explain the chapter's content clearly enough that a learner can tell which canonical topics, tools, cases, mechanisms, or skills belong inside it. A description that only says what the learner will feel or broadly do is too vague if it does not name the actual scope
  - Description filler issue: descriptions should not rely on filler phrases like "explore", "understand", "learn about", "introduction to", or "basics of". Repeated filler phrasing, or descriptions that use these words instead of naming concrete scope, is a minor issue.
  - Warm, plain language throughout. Avoid academic vocabulary
  - Language output must follow the language parameter. For `en`: US English. For `pt`: Brazilian Portuguese. For `es`: Latin American Spanish
  - Titles and descriptions must not leak prompt instructions or make claims about the learner's performance
  - Don't evaluate output format — focus on chapter content quality only

steps.conclusion: The dedicated history chapter is incomplete against the explicit requirements: it does not name the evolution of operating systems, the web, or AI-era changes, and it only weakly addresses major software shifts. The end-to-end delivery chapter also stops at deployment, observability, and incident response without explicitly covering ongoing maintenance and software evolution. These are required academic and practical workflow elements, although the rest of the curriculum is unusually comprehensive and logically sequenced.

Brazilian Portuguese is used consistently overall, but several descriptions rely on avoidable English terms such as “build”, “traces”, “serving”, “drift”, “issues”, “prompting” and “denoising”; some are common technical terms, but Portuguese equivalents or brief clarification would improve accessibility. A few descriptions are also vocabulary-dense for learners outside privileged educational environments.

Expand “História da computação” to explicitly trace operating systems, the web, key software paradigms, and the AI era. Expand the delivery workflow to include post-release maintenance, dependency updates, technical-debt management, user feedback, system evolution, and eventual decommissioning. Briefly define less familiar English jargon when first used to preserve the curriculum’s welcoming tone.

steps.conclusion: The curriculum is exceptionally broad and includes the required standalone biosafety, history, end-to-end workflow, canonical foundations, biodiversity, physiology, ecology, and separate modern-biology topics. However, its practical sequence has material prerequisite inversions: enzyme experiments and other practical activities precede experimental design and biostatistics; microscopy and numerous laboratory activities precede biosafety; and microbial culture appears long after cultivation and identification are already invoked in the bacteria chapter. Biosafety should precede the first laboratory activity, with experimental design introduced before experiments. In addition, the Brazilian regulatory coverage is too generic for a field with professional and safety duties: it mentions Brazilian rules and committees but does not identify key authorization, documentation, reporting, professional-responsibility, and incident procedures or the relevant regulatory structures.

The closing career chapter covers roles, training, registration, experience, portfolio, and staying current, but it does not clearly identify concrete entry and contribution routes or the subject-specific artifacts that demonstrate competence, such as documented protocols, field records, reproducible analyses, reports, datasets, or validated laboratory work. Some descriptions are also dense inventories of specialist vocabulary, which slightly weakens accessibility for the intended audience. “Cultivo biológico e organismos-modelo” combines two scopes that would be clearer as separate chapters or with a more precise microbiological-culture title.

Move biosafety near the beginning, place experimental design and basic statistics before the first experiments, and relocate microbial culture to follow microbiology. Strengthen the Brazilian practice material with named regulatory pathways, documents, committees, reporting duties, and professional responsibilities. The final chapter could more concretely explain employment, community and research entry paths, competence evidence, and continuing education across biological specialties.

steps.conclusion: The required structural-simulation coverage is incomplete. There is one generic finite element analysis chapter, but the outline does not explicitly teach dedicated FEA workflows for suspension components, composite laminates, crash structures, chassis behavior, and lightweight-part optimization. The zero-to-mastery progression also lacks a clear numerical computing, programming, and statistics foundation before CFD, telemetry, optimization, digital twins, and machine learning. Finally, the history sequence compresses the late 1980s–2013 period under “Era turbo,” leaving the naturally aspirated V10/V8 eras and their major safety and regulatory changes insufficiently explicit.

A few titles are overly inventory-like rather than sharply scoped, such as “CAD, arquitetura e empacotamento do carro” and “Segurança estrutural, impactos e Halo.” The history title “Era turbo, eletrônica e aerodinâmica avançada” is also potentially misleading because much of the period through 2013 used naturally aspirated engines.

Add accessible, low-cost practice paths using public telemetry, simplified meshes, open-source numerical tools, spreadsheet models, and small physical experiments so learners outside privileged teams can complete authentic work. The closing chapter could also name communities, mentoring routes, and concrete evidence of competence beyond a general technical portfolio.

steps.conclusion: The curriculum has excellent flagship-service breadth, including standalone Spanner coverage, history, practical work, an end-to-end workflow, and a subject-specific career closing chapter. However, it does not fully support the required zero-knowledge progression. After one overloaded foundations chapter, learners are expected to use CLIs, REST APIs, client libraries, IP addressing, routing, DNS, containers, SQL databases, data pipelines, and machine learning without earlier chapters establishing command-line use, HTTP/API concepts, networking fundamentals such as CIDR, basic application architecture, relational data and SQL, programming, or elementary data/ML concepts. This creates repeated prerequisite gaps and makes the arc less accessible to true beginners.

Several descriptions are dense with unexplained practitioner jargon, including federation, policy inheritance, digests, watermarks, state, DAGs, semantic models, provenance, and recovery objectives. A few umbrella chapters are also less concrete than the rest: “Data transfer and ingestion” does not identify the relevant transfer or migration services, and “Application integration and Workflows” refers vaguely to “managed integration tools.” This slightly weakens legibility and searchability for newcomers.

Add a short sequence of concrete prerequisite chapters before the product-heavy material: command line and APIs, networking fundamentals, application and data fundamentals, and programming/SQL foundations. Then introduce small cumulative labs that reuse one beginner-friendly workload across IAM, networking, compute, storage, databases, deployment, observability, and recovery. Naming specific products in the few vague umbrella descriptions and briefly defining specialized terms when first introduced would further improve accessibility.

steps.conclusion: The quantitative prerequisite arc is incomplete for a stated zero-to-mastery curriculum. The early calculation chapter covers units, notation, angles, and logarithmic scales, but not the algebra, trigonometry, vectors, calculus, differential equations, probability, and core physical models later assumed by orbital fitting, hydrostatic equilibrium, Friedmann cosmology, Bayesian inference, and numerical simulation. Consequently, several advanced chapters depend on knowledge that no earlier chapter clearly teaches.

The programming and astronomical data-files chapter comes after photometry, spectroscopy, data reduction, and astrostatistics, even though those practical chapters normally require the computational tools introduced there. Some instrument terms such as “bias”, “dark”, “flat fields” and “seeing” are used without plain-Spanish clarification. The closing professional chapter covers specialties and staying current, but contribution paths and field-specific evidence of competence are only loosely represented by “redes de colaboración” and “proyectos de portafolio.”

Move programming, FITS handling, reproducible notebooks, and basic statistics ahead of computational observing workflows. Add staged mathematical and physical foundations before the chapters that apply them. Strengthen the closing chapter with concrete entry routes—observatory operations, research assistantships, citizen-science collaborations, instrumentation teams and data archives—and identify evidence such as observing logs, calibrated datasets, reproducible notebooks, proposals, instrument work, and publications.

steps.conclusion: Several explicit major requirements are violated. Many substantial traditions and eras are compressed into survey chapters: “Música académica del siglo XX” combines impressionism, atonality, serialism, neoclassicism, musique concrète, minimalism, and extended techniques; “Jazz moderno” combines bebop through fusion; regional chapters bundle highly distinct African, Asian, Indigenous American, Caribbean, and Latin American traditions; and early rock spans several major movements in one chapter. The progression also has prerequisite problems: jazz transcription and solo analysis precede jazz harmony and improvisation, while electronic-genre production exercises precede DAWs, MIDI, synthesis, audio editing, and mixing. Instrument-family chapters similarly compress multiple distinct instruments and techniques, weakening the promised zero-to-mastery arc. These are material survey and sequencing failures despite the curriculum’s excellent breadth, foundations, history coverage, workflows, and professional-navigation ending.

A few titles and descriptions rely on avoidable English terms where established Spanish wording could improve accessibility, including “performance electrónica,” “sampling,” “beatmatching,” “cueing,” “hooks,” and “fills.” Some are accepted industry terms, so this is mainly a consistency and welcoming-language issue. The curriculum also shifts abruptly between foundational theory, instrument options, long historical coverage, advanced theory, and production rather than clearly marking prerequisite pathways or elective branches.

Split overloaded history and tradition chapters into meaningful eras, genre families, or regional practices, especially twentieth-century concert music, modern jazz, Caribbean music, Indigenous traditions, African traditions, and major Latin American genres. Move prerequisite production chapters before electronic-genre practice, and place harmony, improvisation, and transcription foundations before advanced jazz work. Clarify instrument and professional tracks, with recurring level-appropriate performances, compositions, recordings, and ensemble projects so learners can follow a coherent path toward mastery rather than attempting every instrument family sequentially.

More importantly, neither its title nor description explicitly establishes the foundational distinction among supervised and unsupervised learning before the curriculum moves into their individual methods. This triggers the rubric’s cap for at least one important title hiding canonical terminology, although the later curriculum provides exceptionally strong coverage of both classical and modern machine learning.

  - Foundational chapters are allowed and often necessary, but they must teach concrete concepts, vocabulary, chronology, notation, mental models, or simple skills the learner will use later — not just describe the field from the outside
  - Chapter count is a RESULT of coverage, not a target. Do not penalize or reward specific counts when coverage is justified. Evaluate whether every canonical pillar of the field has at least one chapter — if any pillar is missing, the curriculum is incomplete regardless of count. Broad fields, large platforms, and large fictional canons can legitimately need many chapters. Penalize duplicate, padded, or poorly sequenced chapters, not length by itself
  - MAJOR ERROR: trading breadth for recency. Modern topics must ADD coverage without REPLACING canonical foundations. For example, a machine learning curriculum that covers transformers/LLMs/RAG/diffusion/fine-tuning but drops reinforcement learning, time-series forecasting, recommender systems, or causal inference is incomplete — those are canonical ML pillars. A medicine curriculum that covers telemedicine and modern EMRs but drops anatomy, physiology, pathology, or pharmacology is incomplete. If covering everything needs more chapters, more chapters is the correct answer
  - Must cover BOTH academic AND practical pillars:
    - Academic pillars: theory, foundations, history, canonical body of knowledge
    - Modern toolchain: what working practitioners in this field actually use this year
    - End-to-end workflow: at least one chapter walking through how real work in the field gets delivered start to finish
    - Last-decade shifts: what's been added to the field in the past 10 years that a textbook wouldn't have covered 10 years ago (new regulations, new platforms, new techniques, AI changes, etc.)
  - MAJOR ERROR: for broad fields, professions, sciences, engineering subjects, and serious practices, missing a standalone history/evolution chapter is an academic-pillar gap unless the course itself is already chronological history or the subject is a narrow tool/hobby where history would be padding
  - MAJOR ERROR: for fields, professions, and serious practices, missing a standalone end-to-end workflow chapter is a practical-pillar gap. The workflow should show real work from problem/request/question through decisions, execution, validation, delivery, operation, maintenance, or follow-up, depending on the field
  - For regulated professions and fields with safety duties, local rules, required documents, reporting duties, professional responsibility, and safety practice are curriculum pillars. A generic ethics chapter is not enough when practitioners need separate procedures and judgment
  - The "junior practitioner test": if a junior would be embarrassed on day 1 of real practice because a topic is missing, that topic must exist as its own chapter
  - Cover core pillars before specialized or frontier topics
  - Stay tightly focused on the course title. Generic cross-disciplinary chapters that could be copied unchanged into many unrelated courses are a major error unless the topic IS the subject of the course
  - MAJOR ERROR: meta-chapters that describe, taxonomize, or survey the field from the outside instead of teaching it
  - MAJOR ERROR: survey chapters that list many areas as bullet points without teaching any of them — each topic that matters deserves its own chapter with real depth
  - MAJOR ERROR: catch-all "recent developments" chapters. Titles like "Catch up with the last decade of X", "What's new in X", "Recent developments in X", "The last decade of X", or "Modern X" that bundle multiple distinct modern techniques into a single chapter are a major error. Each modern shift (transformers, LLMs, RAG, diffusion, fine-tuning for ML; CRISPR, sequencing, single-cell biology, spatial omics, synthetic biology for Biology; LGPD/GDPR, digital filing for Law; telemedicine, EMRs for Medicine; etc.) deserves its own substantive chapter
  - Avoid overlap. If two chapters would naturally share most of their lessons, they should be merged or their scopes sharpened
  - Prefer concept names over vendor names in chapter titles/descriptions (e.g., "Package management" instead of a specific tool name). Foundational tools that ARE the subject of the course are an allowed exception. Vendor name usage in non-vendor courses is a minor issue, never a major error
  - For courses covering a field, profession, or serious practice, a "navigating the field" closing chapter is expected. It must cover ROLES AND SPECIALTIES (what kinds of practitioners exist and what they do differently), CONTRIBUTION/ENTRY PATHS (how people actually join — jobs, residencies, open source, communities), the ARTIFACT THAT PROVES COMPETENCE in this field (code repos, case records, clinical hours, recordings), and HOW PRACTITIONERS KEEP SKILLS CURRENT. A narrow "Build a Portfolio" or "Build a Project" closing chapter is a major error — a portfolio is just one small part of navigating a field. The closing chapter must be specific to the subject, not generic career advice
  - For hobby / pop-culture topics, no closing navigation chapter is expected
  - Titles: concise, specific, concrete, natural, learner-facing, and sentence case. Preserve normal capitalization for proper nouns, product names, acronyms, and language-specific conventions. Chapter titles should use the canonical names a serious learner would expect in a normal course outline, especially for searchable field pillars, methods, tools, eras, genres, regulations, standards, and named concepts. Canonical titles such as "Algorithms", "Data structures", "Operating systems", "Cell biology", "Civil procedure", "Backpropagation", "Retrieval-augmented generation", or their natural translated equivalents are good when those are the real chapter topics
  - MAJOR STYLE ISSUE: hiding canonical terms behind practical paraphrases, metaphors, slogans, or "what it does" titles. If a learner scanning or searching for a standard topic would not recognize the chapter because the title avoids the expected term, the title is not good enough. For example, "Informação guardada em bits" is worse than a title that names "Representação de dados"; "O que um computador faz de verdade" is worse than a title that names the actual pillar such as "Arquitetura de computadores" or "Organização de computadores"; "Catch bugs before users do" is worse than "Testing JavaScript code"
  - SCORE CAP: if 3 or more chapter titles hide important canonical/searchable terms that belong in those titles, the majorErrors score must be 7 or lower even when descriptions mention the terms and the underlying coverage is strong. If 1-2 important titles hide canonical/searchable terms, the majorErrors score must be 8 or lower. Descriptions recovering the term do not remove the title problem because users scan and search chapter titles first
  - MAJOR STYLE ISSUE: titles that read like academic catalog entries only because they are bloated, comma-separated inventories, abstract noun stacks, or title-case headlines. Do not penalize a concise canonical course title as "academic" just because it uses the standard term for the topic. Avoid vague, cute, clickbait, or slogan-like titles such as "X Without Fear" unless that phrase is the natural name of the topic. Avoid "I / II / Part 1" — use descriptive subtitles instead
  - Descriptions: 1–2 sentences describing what the chapter covers and, when natural, what it enables. Descriptions should explain the chapter's content clearly enough that a learner can tell which canonical topics, tools, cases, mechanisms, or skills belong inside it. A description that only says what the learner will feel or broadly do is too vague if it does not name the actual scope
  - Description filler issue: descriptions should not rely on filler phrases like "explore", "understand", "learn about", "introduction to", or "basics of". Repeated filler phrasing, or descriptions that use these words instead of naming concrete scope, is a minor issue.
  - Warm, plain language throughout. Avoid academic vocabulary
  - Language output must follow the language parameter. For `en`: US English. For `pt`: Brazilian Portuguese. For `es`: Latin American Spanish
  - Titles and descriptions must not leak prompt instructions or make claims about the learner's performance
  - Don't evaluate output format — focus on chapter content quality only
